old_claim.note: State Armories provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped Vindolanda evidence is a corpus of written Latin documents on wooden tablets, so writing is intrinsic to the record technology.

source_shape.source_locator: The home page describes nearly 780 texts written on thin sheets of wood from the late first and early second centuries at Vindolanda.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The target node is a corpus of written military and administrative documents, not the physical weapon-store institution itself.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Replacing state_armories with writing moves the edge from a stored-goods domain to the actual representational medium required for military depot records.

ontology_before: Military Depot Records depended on state armories, implying the physical arsenal institution was a direct prerequisite for the records node.
ontology_after: Military Depot Records is scoped to written frontier administrative documents; writing carries the medium requirement, while armories are only a possible recorded subject.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: military_depot_records now depends on writing and no longer directly depends on state_armories.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to physical arsenal/depot institutions rather than written records.
A separate Roman arsenal-inventory node is added and should carry the state_armories dependency.
A source shows the scoped Vindolanda record corpus was not written or not documentary.
short_reason: Written records require writing; state armories were an over-specific recorded-goods domain.
